topic: Section 1.1 — Overview of the Ovoid Cavity Cosmological Model
our_argument_summary: Section 1.1 is a descriptive overview that introduces the dome model's geometry, key parameters, and claim count. It presents the dome's architecture (elliptical disc, toroidal ovoid cavity, copper/bronze firmament, aetheric medium, local sun/moon, Polaris at apex), the H(r) exponential decay formula, the B(r) two-pole geomagnetic field equation, key physical parameters (disc semi-axes, sun/moon altitudes, coupling constant κ), and the claim that these produce Earth's dipole field, Schumann resonances, and secular variation from a single parameter set. The TLDR frames the section as 'the review structure, not the geometry, is what makes it interesting to evaluate.'

holes_found.description: Factual arithmetic error: H(r) at equator. The section states 'At the equator (r = 15,000 km), this gives H ≈ 1,270 km.' The actual calculation: H(15000) = 8537 × exp(−15000/8619) = 8537 × 0.1754 ≈ 1,498 km. The stated value of 1,270 km actually corresponds to r ≈ 16,423 km, not 15,000 km — a ~15% error. This is exactly the kind of factual error a dome defender could exploit: 'They can't even evaluate our formula correctly.' An overview section's credibility rests on getting basic math right.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace '1,270 km' with '1,498 km' (or '~1,500 km' for readability). Alternatively, if 1,270 km was intended for a different r value, correct the r value. The fix is a one-word edit in sections.json.

holes_found.description: TLDR hardcodes stale WIN count. The TLDR says 'The model claims 69 confirmed predictions' but {{TOTAL_WINS}} in the body text resolves to 70 (wins.json has 70 entries: WIN-001 through WIN-069 plus WIN-058b). Readers see the TLDR first. A discrepancy between the TLDR and the body count undermines trust — especially ironic given our own criticism of the dome for hardcoding numbers. Per curmudgeon severity guidelines, TLDR factual errors are major because readers may never expand the detail.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the hardcoded '69' in the TLDR with either {{TOTAL_WINS}} (if the template engine supports TLDR substitution) or update it to '70'. Better yet, use a relative phrase like 'dozens of confirmed predictions' that doesn't go stale, since the body text uses the dynamic template for precision.

holes_found.description: B(r) formula sourcing gap. The section presents 'B(r) = 62,376×e^(−r_N/8,619) + 64,852×e^(−r_S/8,619) nT' as if this is a published dome parameter. However, this formula does NOT appear in the dome's model page (raw-text/05-model.txt), home page (raw-text/01-home.txt), or any other extracted raw-text file. It appears to come from the dome's V51.0 toroidal upgrade, which is analyzed in our WIN-053 review but was extracted after the raw-text collection (which is from V50.6, March 2026). A dome defender could argue we're attributing a formula they haven't (or haven't yet) published in the form we cite. While the formula is real (it's in the dome's V51.0 repository), the overview should either cite where it appears on the dome site or note it's from the V51.0 update.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a parenthetical attribution, e.g., '(introduced in V51.0)' after the B(r) formula, or cite the specific dome page/repository location. This also helps readers understand the model's evolution — the two-pole formula replaced V50.6's single-pole model.

holes_found.description: TLDR editorial framing is dismissive. The TLDR ends with 'the review structure, not the geometry, is what makes it interesting to evaluate.' This is an editorial judgment that (a) appears before any evidence is presented, (b) signals we've already concluded the geometry doesn't matter, and (c) could alienate readers who arrived wanting a fair assessment. A dome defender could frame this as prejudging the outcome. The purpose of Section 1.1 is to describe the model — the evaluation belongs in later sections.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the final TLDR sentence with something descriptively accurate, e.g., 'This review examines each claim against published data and the model's own internal consistency.' or 'The following sections evaluate every claimed prediction against independent data.' The editorial judgment can live in Part 9 (Conclusions) where it's earned by evidence.

holes_found.description: 'Ring magnet' attribution. The section says 'This circulation is topologically identical to a ring magnet.' The dome's raw text does not use the phrase 'ring magnet' — it describes a toroidal flow pattern but uses terms like 'Closed Toroidal Ovoid' and 'aetheric medium circulation.' Our characterization is topologically accurate (a toroidal flow IS a ring magnet topology), but presenting it without attribution could be read as us imposing our framing. Since this is our analytical characterization, not the dome's own language, it should be flagged as such.
holes_found.recommendation: Minor wording change: 'This circulation is topologically identical to what physicists call a ring magnet' or add a parenthetical '(our characterization — the dome calls it a "Closed Toroidal Ovoid")'. Low priority.

holes_found.description: Missing 'below detection' nuance. The section says the dome claims '{{TOTAL_WINS}} confirmed predictions and zero falsifications.' The dome's home page distinguishes between 'Falsified' (0) and 'Below Detection' (2), treating the latter as noise-limited null results rather than model failures. While our text is technically accurate — the dome does claim zero falsifications — omitting the 'below detection' category in an overview section could be seen as incomplete. The dome defender would say we're hiding the fact that the dome acknowledges two inconclusive results.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief parenthetical: '...zero falsifications (with two results classified as "below detection threshold").' This shows completeness and fairness — we're not hiding anything the dome says.

stronger_arguments.description: The overview could note the dome's own internal H(r) inconsistency. The dome's model page says 'H at equatorial rim: ~2,100 km' but H(r=15000) = 1,498 km and H(r=20015) = 837 km — neither matches 2,100 km (which corresponds to r ≈ 12,088 km). This internal arithmetic inconsistency could be mentioned as a preview of the precision issues explored in later sections.
sources: raw-text/05-model.txt line 36 ('H at equatorial rim: ~2,100 km')

stronger_arguments.description: The overview could highlight that the dome's 'single set of parameters' claim is undermined by the shared decay constant λ = 8,619 km appearing in both H(r) and B(r). This is presented as elegant unification, but it's actually a red flag: there's no physical reason a cavity's height profile and its magnetic field should share the same exponential decay length. Section 6.8 covers this in detail, but a one-sentence preview here would prime the reader.

kernel_of_truth.description: The overview is genuinely fair in its description of the dome model. It presents the dome's architecture and parameters without mockery or strawmanning. The explanation of the H(r) exponential decay, the toroidal flow, and the coupling constant is accurate and would likely satisfy the dome author that his model is being represented correctly. This is exactly the right tone for an introductory section.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Being fair in the overview doesn't validate the dome model — it validates our review methodology. The overview's neutrality strengthens our credibility precisely because the evidence-based criticism in later sections hits harder when readers trust we understood the model first.

deeper_analysis:
Section 1.1 is structurally sound as an overview — it describes the model faithfully, sets up the evaluation framework, and correctly uses {{TOTAL_WINS}} template in the body (avoiding hardcoded counts). The two substantive errors (H(r) arithmetic, stale TLDR count) are both easily fixable but both are embarrassing if caught by a dome defender. The B(r) sourcing issue is more subtle: the formula IS the dome's equation, but our raw-text extractions predate V51.0 where it was introduced, so we can't currently point to our own source documentation. A dome defender who reads our raw-text/ directory could argue we fabricated the formula. The fix is simple: either update the raw-text extraction to include V51.0 content, or add an explicit V51.0 attribution in the overview text.

The TLDR's editorial tone ('the review structure, not the geometry, is what makes it interesting to evaluate') is a judgment call. On one hand, it signals to readers that the review is about methodology and evidence, not about attacking flat earth per se. On the other hand, it prejudges the outcome — telling readers the geometry doesn't matter before showing why. The strongest reviews earn their conclusions; they don't announce them in the first paragraph.

citations_unchecked: No DOIs or paper citations in Section 1.1 — it's purely descriptive

tldr_review.tldr_issues: Hardcoded '69' contradicts {{TOTAL_WINS}} = 70. Final sentence is editorial judgment rather than description.
